metadata.summary: Five viral loop archetypes (WOM, Inherent, Incentivized, Content, Outbreak) with K-factor ranges, share mechanics, product fit conditions, and selection guide. Never run 4 loops simultaneously.

content.title: Viral Loops and Types
content.summary: Five viral loop archetypes — Word of Mouth, Inherent, Incentivized, Content, and Outbreak — each with distinct K-factor ranges, share mechanics, and product fit conditions. Pick the loop type that matches the product's natural use, then layer one secondary loop. Never run 4 loops simultaneously.

why.p: Loop type selection determines the ceiling of achievable K. Inherent virality (Slack, Zoom) can reach K=2+ because collaboration requires inviting others. Content virality (TikTok) has K=0.3-0.8 because conversion from viewer to signup is low. Choosing the wrong loop type (forcing "inherent" onto a non-collaborative product) kills NPS without raising K.

list.item: Choosing between viral loop types for a new product or vertical.
Modeling growth plans: need realistic K, i, c benchmarks before committing to a target.
Trying to raise K by 0.1-0.3 and needing a structured experiment plan.
Instrumenting share-moment events for weekly K reporting by loop type.
You want an agent to instrument share-moment events and report loop health weekly.
Pre-retention: virality on a leaky bucket just speeds churn. Prove D30 retention first.
B2B enterprise with 6-month sales cycles — loops do not compound on that timescale.
Heavily regulated products (medical, financial) where outbound viral mechanics breach compliance.
When you have fewer than 500 invite events — K is noise at small samples.

section.name: loop-mechanics
section.p: All viral loops share the same 5-step cycle: user joins → encounters trigger → sends invitation → friend converts → new user repeats the cycle. Loop type determines where the trigger occurs and how strong it is.

rule.statement: Use WOM when the product is genuinely exceptional and natural share moments exist at peak satisfaction; do not engineer WOM for mediocre products.
rule.rationale: WOM has low i (not everyone tells friends) but high c (personal recommendation converts strongly). Forcing WOM prompts on a product without real delight backfires — users feel pressured and NPS drops.

list.item: Typical K: 0.1-0.5
Examples: Notion (early), Figma (design teams)
Best for: productivity tools, exceptional consumer products, strong brand

rule.statement: Use inherent virality only when the product functionally requires others to participate; do not force collaboration features onto solo-use products.
rule.rationale: Inherent virality has the highest K ceiling because the invite is required, not optional. Forced inherent virality (fake "invite to see content") gets labeled spam and damages NPS.

list.item: Typical K: 0.5-2.0
Examples: Slack, Zoom, Figma, Google Docs
Best for: collaboration, team, and multi-player products

rule.statement: Use incentivized virality when LTV is greater than 3x CAC and the reward can be sized at 20-50% of CAC without destroying margins; implement double-sided incentives.
rule.rationale: Incentivized virality boosts i but reduces authenticity, lowering c. The net K is viable when the reward economics work. Single-sided referrals feel exploitative and perform 20-30% worse.

list.item: Typical K: 0.3-1.0
Examples: Dropbox (free storage), Uber ($10 credit), PayPal ($10 cash)
Best for: high-LTV SaaS, fintech, e-commerce with margin room

rule.statement: Use content virality when users naturally produce shareable artifacts (scores, results, badges, created content) that embed product branding without feeling like an ad.
rule.rationale: Content virality has variable i (depends on content quality and shareability) and low c (many views, few signups). The share artifact must have novelty and social value — most imitations fail.

list.item: Typical K: 0.1-0.5 (content platforms can hit 0.8+)
Examples: TikTok, Wordle (K=1.5+), Spotify Wrapped
Best for: content creation platforms, social apps, entertainment products

rule.statement: Do not implement outbreak (automated contact import) virality; modern email filters and OS protections block it, and it damages brand reputation.
rule.rationale: Outbreak virality automates invites to full contact lists. It was viable in 1996 (Hotmail email signature). Today it triggers spam filters, breaches CAN-SPAM, and is rejected by app stores.

list.item: Collaboration tool: Inherent (primary) + WOM (secondary)
Consumer app: Content (primary) + WOM (secondary)
Marketplace: Incentivized (primary)
Productivity tool: WOM (primary) + Inherent (secondary if team features exist)
B2B SaaS: WOM (primary) + Incentivized (secondary)
Gaming: Content (primary) + WOM (secondary)

rule.statement: Run at most 2 loops in parallel; instrument them with distinct loop_type property on all invite events so K can be computed per loop separately.
rule.rationale: Attribution between loops is messy without explicit tagging. Running 4 loops at once makes it impossible to know which is contributing and which is wasting engineering effort.

list.item: WOM: user achieves their goal; user discovers unexpected value; user solves a major problem
Inherent: user needs collaboration; user wants to share work; user requires team input
Incentivized: user completes onboarding; user reaches usage milestone; user is on referral page
Content: user creates something; user achieves a result; user wants recognition/comparison

rule.statement: Instrument invite_sent with loop_type, share_channel, and recipient_count properties before launch; without these, K cannot be diagnosed when it drops.
rule.rationale: K drops silently. A redesigned share button can drop i by 40% without any alert. Weekly K split by loop_type and share_channel is the minimum for actionable monitoring.

list.item: LLMs love to recommend "make it inherent" for non-collaborative products — forced inherent virality kills NPS.
Outbreak/spam loops still get suggested by older sources; modern email and OS protections kill them and damage brand.
Wordle-style content virality is survivorship-biased; most copies fail because the share artifact lacks novelty.
K is volatile early; sample size for K significance is much larger than for typical conversion tests.
Loop type is rarely pure — most products run 2-3 loops in parallel and attribution between them is messy.

antipattern.description: Agent recommends "make it inherent" for a non-collaborative product.
antipattern.reason: Forced inherent virality creates fake invite prompts that users reject. It kills NPS without raising K. Inherent virality only works when collaboration is the core value proposition.

antipattern.description: Computing K on fewer than 500 invite events and reporting it as meaningful.
antipattern.reason: K is volatile at small samples. Signal:noise ratio is unacceptable below ~500 invites per reporting period. Agent must flag low-power computations explicitly.

antipattern.description: Recommending outbreak/spam loops based on outdated case studies.
antipattern.reason: Pre-2005 outbreak mechanics are blocked by modern email filters and mobile OS permissions. Always reject this suggestion regardless of source.

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: On opus, a strategist agent picks 1-2 loop types per product type, drafts share-moment events, and models target K. A sonnet agent writes share-flow copy and designs the artifact (image, link preview, OG meta). A haiku ops agent computes i, c, K weekly from analytics, splits by loop and segment, and posts a delta vs previous week to a dashboard or Slack channel.

Recommended subagents: faion-growth-agent (opus) — loop selection, K modeling, growth simulation. Copy/sonnet subagent — share copy, artifact design brief, OG metadata. Data/haiku subagent — i, c, K computation per loop, anomaly detection.

prompt.purpose: Loop type recommendation with K modeling

section.prompt: Given product = {description}, user count = {N}, retention curve = {curve}, recommend 1-2 viral loop types. For each: target K, top 3 share moments, required instrumentation events, expected time to K=0.5. Cite which benchmark range you used.

prompt.purpose: Weekly K computation per loop type

section.prompt: Compute i, c, K for last 14d split by loop_type in {events_table}. Compare to prior 14d. Flag any loop where K dropped >20%. Return markdown table + one-line diagnosis per loop.

list.item: posthog SDK — Open-source product analytics, custom K dashboards (pip install posthog)
mixpanel SDK — Funnel + cohort for invite to signup conversion (pip install mixpanel)
amplitude Data API — i/c/K via JQL or behavioral cohorts (https://amplitude.com/docs/apis)
segment CLI — Route share events to multiple destinations (npm i -g @segment/cli)
branch / appsflyer — Mobile deep-link attribution for invite flows
Branch: SaaS, Yes (REST) — Deep links, cross-platform invite attribution
AppsFlyer: SaaS, Yes (REST) — Same, larger enterprise focus
PostHog: OSS, Yes (REST + self-host) — Best for agent-driven analysis on owned infra
Mixpanel: SaaS, Yes (REST + JQL) — Mature behavioral cohorts
Amplitude: SaaS, Yes (REST) — Strongest funnel/cohort UX
Customer.io: SaaS, Yes (REST) — Trigger invite emails on share-moment events

example.title: viral_sim.py — agent uses to compare loop choices
example.code:
# viral_sim.py — agent uses to compare loop choices def simulate(initial: int, k: float, cycle_days: int, days: int) -> int: cycles = days // cycle_days users = initial for _ in range(cycles): users = users + int(users * k) return users
# Compare 30d for K=0.4 (good WOM) vs K=0.8 (incentivized) # print(simulate(1000, 0.4, 7, 30)) # ~3,800 # print(simulate(1000, 0.8, 7, 30)) # ~10,500

list.item: Pick the loop type that matches the product's natural use, then layer one secondary loop. Don't try to run 4 loops at once.
Instrument the share moment as a first-class event with loop_type, share_channel, recipient_count properties before launch.
Treat K below 0.5 as a supplement, not a strategy — pair with paid or content acquisition.
Optimize cycle time as aggressively as K — halving cycle time roughly equals doubling K in compounding terms.
Re-baseline K quarterly; product changes silently break loops (a redesigned share button can drop i by 40%).

antipattern.description: Models confuse K with conversion rate; force the prompt to specify K = i times c.
antipattern.reason: K is the product of average invites per user (i) and invitee conversion rate (c). Conflating K with c leads to wrong optimization decisions.

antipattern.description: Agent pushing outbreak (auto-contact-import) loops without explicit human approval.
antipattern.reason: TOS and CAN-SPAM landmine. Outbreak mechanics are dead on modern email and mobile platforms.

antipattern.description: Weekly K computed on fewer than 500 invites reported as meaningful signal.
antipattern.reason: K is noise below ~500 invites per reporting period; agent should flag low-power computations rather than report them.

antipattern.description: Agents over-fit on Dropbox/PayPal case studies when recommending a target K.
antipattern.reason: Require it to cite a benchmark range (consumer apps: 0.1-0.3; B2B SaaS: 0.05-0.2) before recommending a target K.

antipattern.description: Mobile attribution agents using different client_id across web and app.
antipattern.reason: Passing different IDs silently breaks K attribution. Use the same client_id consistently across all platforms.

template.purpose: Python simulator comparing loop choices over N days
